timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Esther Saves Her People

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — Esther Reveals Her Identity and Haman’s Plot

heb.paragraph: The adversary and enemy is this vile Haman. In one sentence Esther unmasks herself (Jewish) and Haman (enemy). The double revelation is the climax.

hist.historical: The second banquet follows the sleepless night and Haman’s humiliation. The king is primed; Esther’s timing is providential.
hist.context: Esther reveals everything simultaneously: she is Jewish, her people are condemned, the architect is present. She frames it as a threat to the king’s own queen, making it personal before political.

refs.note: Nathan’s unmasking — the guilty party present when the trap springs.

notes.note: The king asks for the third time. Each repetition escalates commitment.
Grant me my life — the queen herself is condemned. The king’s decree would kill his own wife.
Who is he? Where is he? The king’s fury is immediate.
The adversary and enemy is this vile Haman. Haman is terrified.

Calvin: Esther revealed her nation only when the moment demanded it. Concealment was prudence; revelation was obedience to the hour. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

Calvin: one word — Haman — and the mighty counsellor is ruined. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes Esther avoids directly blaming the king, preserving his dignity while exposing the crime. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

The NET observes "was terrified" uses the same verb as Exodus 15:15 for the nations’ terror. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

levenson.source: Jon D. Levenson, Esther, OTL (1997) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Levenson: as Moses confronted Pharaoh, Esther confronts Haman. The intercession is from within, not from above. Levenson's literary reading situates the text within the Persian-period context that produced it. The narrative's themes of divine providence, diaspora identity, and reversal of fortune speak directly to the experience of Jewish communities navigating life under foreign rule while maintaining covenant faithfulness.

Levenson: the adversary is the book’s vocabulary for cosmic enmity. The Agagite meets his end. Levenson's literary reading situates the text within the Persian-period context that produced it. The narrative's themes of divine providence, diaspora identity, and reversal of fortune speak directly to the experience of Jewish communities navigating life under foreign rule while maintaining covenant faithfulness.

jobes.source: Karen H. Jobes, Esther, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes: Esther makes it about the king’s loss. Your queen would die carries more weight than a distant people would die. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

Jobes: three words end all concealment. Esther is Jewish. Haman is the enemy. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

sections.header: Verses 7–10 — Haman Falls; Hanged on His Own Gallows

heb.paragraph: They hanged Haman on the gallows he prepared for Mordecai. The poetic justice is exact. The gallows is the book’s supreme symbol of reversal.

hist.historical: Covering the face was the Persian signal for a condemned man. Harbona’s mention of the gallows provides immediate justice.
hist.context: The reversal is complete. Every intention boomerangs. The book’s structure is a mirror: everything reflects back to its source.

refs.note: His mischief returns upon his own head.
Daniel’s accusers thrown to the lions — same pattern.

notes.note: The king storms out. Haman stays to plead with Esther.
The king interprets Haman’s desperate plea as assault. The misinterpretation seals the sentence.
Harbona: a pole stands by Haman’s house, prepared for Mordecai. The instrument is ready.
Then the king’s fury subsided. Justice calms wrath.

Calvin: he was hanged on the gallows he built. The wicked are destroyed by their own devices. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes covering the face is a Persian execution custom: irrevocable condemnation. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

The NET observes the pattern echoes Proverbs: the pit-digger falls in. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

levenson.source: Jon D. Levenson, Esther, OTL (1997) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Levenson: the reversal is the governing concept. What was planned for the Jews happens to their enemy. Levenson's literary reading situates the text within the Persian-period context that produced it. The narrative's themes of divine providence, diaspora identity, and reversal of fortune speak directly to the experience of Jewish communities navigating life under foreign rule while maintaining covenant faithfulness.

jobes.source: Karen H. Jobes, Esther, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes: Haman killed on his own gallows. The symmetry is too perfect for chance. The structure speaks for itself. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

ppl.name: Harbona
ppl.text: One of the seven eunuchs from chapter 1. His well-timed mention provides the king with immediate justice.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVand as they were drinking wine on the second day, the king again asked, “Queen Esther, what is your petition? It will be given you. What is your request? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be granted.”ESVAnd on the second day, as they were drinking wine after the feast, the king again said to Esther, “What is your wish, Queen Esther? It shall be granted you. And what is your request? Even to the half of my kingdom, it shall be fulfilled.”NIVFor I and my people have been sold to be destroyed, killed and annihilated. If we had merely been sold as male and female slaves, I would have kept quiet, because no such distress would justify disturbing the king.”ESVFor we have been sold, I and my people, to be destroyed, to be killed, and to be annihilated. If we had been sold merely as slaves, men and women, I would have been silent, for our affliction is not to be compared with the loss to the king.”

rec.text: "The king asks for the third time. Each repetition escalates commitment."

rows.label: The Unmasking — 7:1–6
rows.text: I am Jewish. Haman is the enemy. Double concealment ended in one sentence.

rows.label: The Fall — 7:7–10
rows.text: Own gallows, own plot, own destruction. The supreme reversal.

hebtext.note: The adversary and enemy is this vile Haman. In one sentence Esther unmasks herself (Jewish) and Haman (enemy). The double revelation is the climax.
They hanged Haman on the gallows he prepared for Mordecai. The poetic justice is exact. The gallows is the book’s supreme symbol of reversal.

thread.text: Nathan’s unmasking — the guilty party present when the trap springs.
His mischief returns upon his own head.
Daniel’s accusers thrown to the lions — same pattern.

themes.note: Sovereignty and justice both peak at 10. The reversal is the theological argument.
